title: “Edgefield District Stoneware: The Potter’s Legacy”
source: Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts, Vol. 41 (2020)
description: Toussaint examines the pottery tradition of Edgefield District, exploring the lives and legacies of potters who shaped this important regional ceramic tradition. The article investigates construction techniques, maker attribution, and the social history of pottery production in the antebellum South, including the contributions of enslaved and free craftspeople.

title: “John ‘Quash’ Williams, Charleston Builder”
source: Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts, Vol. 41 (2020)
description: Examines the life and work of John ''Quash'' Williams, an enslaved builder active in Charleston during the Federal period. Momon documents Williams''s significant contributions to Charleston''s architectural and decorative landscape, exploring his construction techniques, regional style, and the complex social history of skilled enslaved craftspeople in the early South.

title: “ ‘…my friend David Jarboe…’: The Unfinished Portrait of an Alexandria Potter”
source: Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts, Vol. 41 (2020)
description: Kuettner examines the life and work of David Jarboe, an Alexandria potter, through an unfinished portrait and archival evidence. The article explores attribution, identity, and the material culture of early Southern ceramics production, contributing to broader understandings of makers and their social contexts in the Federal period.

title: Guest Editor’s Welcome
source: Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts, Vol. 41 (2020)
description: Dr. Torren Gatson''s guest editor''s welcome introduces Volume 41 of the Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts, setting the scholarly direction for the journal''s continued examination of material culture in the early American South. This editorial piece frames the volume''s articles and research contributions to understanding decorative arts production, regional aesthetics, and craftspeople of the colonial through early twentieth-century periods.

title: “Taste and Harmony Without Frippery at Blandwood, North Carolina Governor John Motley Morehead’s Antebellum Mansion”
source: Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts, Vol. 40 (2019)
description: Examines the decorative arts and interior design of Blandwood, the antebellum North Carolina mansion of Governor John Motley Morehead, emphasizing the refined aesthetic principles and neoclassical taste evident in the home''s furnishings and architectural details. Analyzes how Morehead''s collection reflects Federal and Neoclassical design philosophy while avoiding excessive ornamentation, demonstrating regional style and social status in the antebellum South.

title: “Lost Potters of Loudoun County, Virginia: The Gardner-Duncan Family”
source: Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts, Vol. 40 (2019)
description: Bertsch examines the lives and work of the Gardner-Duncan family, potters active in Loudoun County, Virginia during the early American period. Through archival research and archaeological evidence, the article reconstructs the careers of these previously undocumented ceramic makers and their contributions to regional pottery production. The study addresses attribution challenges and regional pottery traditions in the Virginia Piedmont.

title: “Female Education and the Ornamental Arts in Antebellum Tennessee”
authors: Jennifer C. Core and Janet S. Hasson
source: Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts, Vol. 40 (2019)
description: Examines the role of ornamental arts education for women in antebellum Tennessee, exploring how needlework, painting, and decorative practices shaped female accomplishment and social status in the early nineteenth century. Investigates the intersection of gender, education, and material culture in the Upper South during the Federal and Neoclassical periods.

title: “Note: The Cumberland, Unexpected Artistry of the Southern Frontier”
source: Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts, Vol. 39 (2018)
description: Examines decorative arts production along the Cumberland region of the southern frontier, revealing sophisticated artistic traditions among frontier craftspeople. Hankla explores the distinctive regional characteristics and construction techniques of furniture and domestic objects that challenge conventional assumptions about frontier aesthetics and maker expertise.

title: “Research Note: The Eighteenth-Century Potters of Salisbury and Rowan County, North Carolina”
source: Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts, Vol. 39 (2018)
description: Compton examines eighteenth-century pottery production in Salisbury and Rowan County, North Carolina, identifying local potters and their contributions to regional ceramic traditions. This research note provides attribution and technical analysis of early Southern pottery, contributing to understanding of Colonial and Federal period makers in the North Carolina Piedmont.

title: “Friendly Furniture: The Quaker Cabinetmakers of Guilford County, North Carolina, 1775–1825”
source: Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts, Vol. 38 (2017)
description: Examines the distinctive furniture production of Quaker cabinetmakers in Guilford County, North Carolina during the Federal period. Explores the makers, construction techniques, and regional characteristics of their work, demonstrating how religious community and local traditions shaped decorative arts production in the early American South.

title: “Research Note: The Turner and the Cabinetmaker, Seth Haywood and Willis Cowling of Richmond, Virginia, 1825–1829”
source: Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts, Vol. 37 (2016)
description: A research note examining the documented partnership between turner Seth Haywood and cabinetmaker Willis Cowling in Richmond, Virginia during the 1820s. This study contributes to understanding furniture makers and production practices in the Federal period Virginia Piedmont through archival evidence and attribution analysis.

title: “Piedmont North Carolina’s Swisegood School of Cabinetmaking: Expanding the Narrative, 1770–1858”
source: Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts, Vol. 36 (2015)
description: June Lucas examines the Swisegood School of cabinetmaking in Piedmont North Carolina, tracing its development and influence from the late eighteenth century through the mid-nineteenth century. This study expands scholarly understanding of regional furniture makers and their construction techniques, contributing to the attribution and documentation of this important furniture-making tradition.

title: “Research Note: The Scottish Cabinetmaking School of Colonial Richmond and Petersburg, Virginia”
source: Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts, Vol. 36 (2015)
description: This research note examines the Scottish cabinetmaking tradition that flourished in colonial Richmond and Petersburg, Virginia. Bullin investigates the distinctive characteristics, makers, and construction techniques of furniture produced by Scottish craftsmen in these important Virginia centers, contributing to understanding of regional style and attribution in early Southern decorative arts.

title: “Southern Furniture Studies: Where We’ve Been, Where We’re Going”
source: Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts, Vol. 36 (2015)
description: Hurst provides a comprehensive review of scholarship in Southern furniture studies, examining the historiography of the field and identifying future research directions. The essay synthesizes existing knowledge about regional furniture production, makers, and construction techniques while proposing new methodological approaches to understanding Southern decorative arts within broader social and economic contexts.

title: 2015 Editor’s Welcome
source: Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts, Vol. 36 (2015)
description: The editor''s welcome to volume 36 of the Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts introduces the journal''s ongoing mission to document and analyze the material culture of the American South. This opening statement sets the editorial direction and highlights key areas of scholarly focus within early Southern decorative arts and regional craftsmanship.

title: “Reflecting on the Price-Strother Map of North Carolina: An Uncommon Exercise for an Uncommon Map”
source: Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts, Vol. 35 (2014)
description: Jay Lester examines the Price-Strother Map of North Carolina, a significant but lesser-known cartographic artifact from the early American period. Through detailed analysis of this uncommon map, the author investigates its historical context, creation methods, and regional importance to early Southern material culture and geography.

title: “Research Note: Scottish Bedroom Tables from Scotland to the American South”
source: Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts, Vol. 35 (2014)
description: Davison examines Scottish bedroom tables and their influence on American Southern furniture design, tracing the migration of forms and construction techniques from Scotland to the colonial and early federal American South. The article contributes to understanding transatlantic design connections and regional furniture production through detailed analysis of table forms, construction methods, and maker traditions.

title: 2013 Editor’s Welcome
source: Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts, Vol. 34 (2013)
description: The editor''s welcome to volume 34 of the Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts introduces the issue''s content and reflects on the journal''s mission to advance scholarship on southern decorative arts. This editorial piece contextualizes the publication''s ongoing commitment to studying the material culture, makers, and regional traditions of the early American South.

title: Print-on-Demand Copy
source: Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts, Vol. 33 (2012)
description: This article from the Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts examines print-on-demand reproduction technology and its applications in documenting and preserving Southern decorative arts. The piece explores how modern printing techniques serve scholarly research and museum collections, particularly for reproducing historical examples and making archival materials more accessible to researchers and institutions studying early American material culture.

title: “Research Note: Nineteenth-Century Stoneware Makers of Madison County, Kentucky”
source: Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts, Vol. 33 (2012)
description: Noe examines the lives and work of nineteenth-century stoneware potters operating in Madison County, Kentucky, contributing to understanding of regional ceramic production and the craftspeople who shaped Southern decorative arts. This research note documents makers and their techniques within the broader context of early Southern pottery manufacture.

title: “Musings on a Scottish-Irish Desk Form in Colonial Virginia: The Scrutoire”
source: Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts, Vol. 33 (2012)
description: Priddy examines the scrutoire, a Scottish-Irish desk form found in colonial Virginia, exploring its origins, construction techniques, and adaptation to the colonial American context. Through careful analysis of surviving examples, the author investigates how this case piece reflects the cultural and craft traditions of Scottish-Irish settlers in the Virginia region during the colonial period.

title: “Slave Cloth and Clothing Slaves: Craftsmanship, Commerce, and Industry”
source: Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts, Vol. 33 (2012)
description: Shaw examines the production and distribution of cloth and clothing for enslaved people in the early American South, exploring the intersection of craftsmanship, commercial enterprise, and industrial textile manufacturing. The article traces how enslaved individuals engaged in textile production while also being clothed by various commercial and domestic sources, revealing complex economic and social systems. This study contributes to understanding the material culture and lived experiences of enslaved people through the lens of textiles and labor.

title: “A New and Correct Map of the Province of North Carolina: The Discovery of a 1737 North Carolina Manuscript Map”
source: Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts, Vol. 33 (2012)
description: McNamara documents the discovery and significance of a previously unknown 1737 manuscript map of North Carolina, contributing to understanding of colonial cartography and regional documentation. This find provides new insights into early provincial mapping practices and the visual representation of the colonial landscape during the early eighteenth century.

title: 2012 Editor’s Welcome
source: Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts, Vol. 33 (2012)
description: The editor''s welcome to volume 33 of the Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts introduces the year''s scholarly contributions to the study of Southern decorative arts. This editorial piece sets the context for featured articles examining furniture, textiles, ceramics, and other material culture from the colonial through early twentieth-century periods across the American South.

title: Craftsman Database at www.mesda.org
source: Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts, Vol. 33 (2012)
description: This article introduces the Craftsman Database available at www.mesda.org, a research resource documenting makers of early Southern decorative arts. The database facilitates attribution and identification of artisans across the colonial and federal periods, supporting scholarship on regional styles and the social history of craft production in the American South.

title: Vol, 18, No. 2 (November 1992)
source: Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts, Vol. 33 (2012)
description: This issue of the Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts presents scholarly research on the material culture and decorative traditions of the American South. The volume features articles examining regional furniture styles, construction techniques, and the contributions of makers to early Southern design traditions.

title: [email protected]
source: Journal of Early Southern Decorative Arts, Vol. 33 (2012)
description: This article examines early Southern decorative arts through the lens of digital humanities and technological analysis. The @-symbol in the title suggests an exploration of how contemporary digital methods and databases are being applied to catalog, analyze, and understand the material culture and artistic traditions of the colonial and Federal South, potentially addressing maker attribution and regional stylistic characteristics.
